summary: A complete fan-created Battlestar Galactica Seder that maps ritual objects, cups, exile, cyclical history, guilt, and fellowship onto the television universe while also borrowing Sanskrit prayers. It is a meaningful example of playful adaptation but must remain an explicit fandom opt-in with separate franchise, quotation, and interfaith review.

sourceAssessment.voice: Playful, referential, adult-fandom, participatory, allegorical, irreverent in places, and intentionally dependent on knowledge of Battlestar Galactica.
bestUses: An explicit fan-theme demonstration of how a Haggadah can be adapted
Optional readings about cyclical history, responsibility for what people create, and fellowship
A research example for playful ritual remapping rather than a default Haggadah spine
beginnerRisks: The jokes, characters, invented foods, and plot references are opaque to non-fans.
Franchise quotations and character material require rights review even though the compiled Haggadah is openly licensed.
Sanskrit prayers must be named as interfaith borrowing and never presented as Jewish liturgy.
Fictional violence and survival language can overwhelm the actual Passover story if not clearly framed.
sourceAssessment.politicalContext: The source’s war, displacement, extinction, and enemy language refers to a fictional universe. It must not be mapped onto Israelis, Palestinians, Jews, Muslims, Arabs, or any contemporary conflict, and it cannot displace the app’s peace-focused editorial policy.
sourceAssessment.creditContext: Credit David Lieberman, Alison Ogden, Mary Bruch, Aharon N. Varady, and Open Siddur; preserve CC BY-SA 4.0 and GFDL provenance, require explicit fandom opt-in, and review franchise quotations and embedded prayers separately.

recommendedSequences.title: Cyclical history and an interfaith opening
recommendedSequences.beginnerContext: State that this is an opt-in Battlestar fan adaptation, explain the cyclical-history premise, and label the Gâyatrî Mantra as a Hindu text rather than Jewish liturgy.
recommendedSequences.rationale: The source’s first two records establish its recurring-history lens and complete the deliberately cross-cultural opening before the plate ritual begins.
recommendedSequences.creditNotes: Credit all four compilers and Open Siddur; separately verify franchise quotation and Gâyatrî Mantra attribution.

recommendedSequences.title: Fan-remapped plate and invitation
recommendedSequences.beginnerContext: Explain the ordinary parsley, salt water, matzah, and invitation first, then show how the fan text playfully remaps each element to fictional exile and survival.
recommendedSequences.rationale: These adjacent records keep the plate symbolism, ritual eating, invitation to those in need, and wish for peace together as one adapted ritual movement.
recommendedSequences.creditNotes: Credit Lieberman, Ogden, Bruch, Varady, and Open Siddur; preserve open-license provenance and review franchise references.

recommendedSequences.title: Second cup and fictional migration story
recommendedSequences.beginnerContext: Give the real Exodus story elsewhere in full; introduce this only as the fan Haggadah’s fictional second-cup story about exile, migration, danger, and searching for home.
recommendedSequences.rationale: Three consecutive records preserve the second-cup toast and the full Battlestar migration narrative without confusing it with the project’s foundational Exodus account.
recommendedSequences.creditNotes: Credit all compilers and Open Siddur; review quoted and paraphrased franchise story material separately.

recommendedSequences.title: Third cup and responsibility for creation
recommendedSequences.beginnerContext: Introduce this as a fictional ethical meditation: the characters face responsibility for creating life and for the consequences they tried to disown.
recommendedSequences.rationale: The paired records set up the third cup as guilt and complete the source’s argument about moral responsibility for what people create.
recommendedSequences.creditNotes: Credit all compilers and Open Siddur and review any direct television dialogue before runtime approval.

recommendedSequences.title: Fellowship and interfaith closing
recommendedSequences.beginnerContext: Name the fourth cup as fellowship, identify the closing Sanskrit prayer as an Upanishadic text, and explain that the final destination is fictional Earth.
recommendedSequences.rationale: The source’s final ritual records move from responsibility to the fleet into a prayer for truth, light, and life and then the fan adaptation’s closing refrain.
recommendedSequences.creditNotes: Credit the compilers and Open Siddur; verify the Upanishadic prayer translation and franchise material separately.

reasons: Character-counting song is highly spoiler-dependent and begins a run of dated insulting character descriptions.
Dated gendered and insulting character descriptions are unsuitable for the project’s playful-but-respectful tone.
Body-shaming and insulting character labels are unsuitable for contemporary guest-facing use.
Continuation of the dated character-label sequence is not needed for a coherent fandom demonstration.
Preservation history, contributor notes, and license material belong in provenance and credits rather than the ritual reading stream.

notes: This source is included rather than erased, but every use must be triggered by an explicit Battlestar Galactica choice.
The real Exodus narrative and novice ritual instructions remain mandatory even when a fandom sequence is selected.
Open licensing of the compilation does not automatically clear embedded franchise dialogue, plot material, or third-party prayer translations.
All selected sequences remain candidate-only pending human rights and editorial review.
